coredata.dc:title: Mechanistic studies on the β-resorcylic acid mediated carbon dots for the pH-induced fluorescence switch and sensing application
coredata.dc:creator: Moniruzzaman, Md
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) responsive to external stimuli that cause changes in their optical properties are in high demand. Herein, we present a microwave synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs derived for the first time from β-resorcylic acid (henceforth referred as R-CDs) in sulfuric acid (H2SO4) medium. Interestingly, the as-synthesized R-CDs showed tunable optical properties with three distinguishable colors (green/blue/blue-violet) depending on pH under UV irradiation. Spectral analyses and mechanistic studies revealed that the fluorescence switch is due to the deprotonation of the oxygenous surface functionalities in the R-CDs. The pH-dependent color switchability was tested in a number of cycles under UV light by adding 0.1 (M) HCl and NaOH. Although several mechanisms involving the complex photoluminescence of CDs have been reported, we propose here that the photoluminescence behavior of the CDs can be tuned by deprotonation of the oxygenous surface functionalities. Moreover, these R-CDs can be used as a potential visual indicator of three different pH ranges, and as highly selective and sensitive sensing medium for Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.43 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Deprotonation
Fluorescence switch mechanism
pH indicator
Photoluminescence quenching
Fe3+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation mechanism and morphological analysis of R-CDs
pH-dependent fluorescence switching and its probable mechanism
Detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy
Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Trade , Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the GRRC program of Gyeonggi Province (GRRC Gachon 2017-B01 ), and by the Human Resource Development of the Korea Insitute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade , Industry and Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea ( 20174030201530 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM images (inset shows HR-TEM image), (c) Gaussian size distribution, and (d) XRD pattern of as-prepared R-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission of sulphuric acid treated R-CDs before and after microwave treatment; (b) PL excitation-dependent emission spectra of R-CDs in DI water at pH 7 ± 0.1.
(a) FT-IR spectra of β-resorcylic acid and R-CDs at pH 7, (b) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectrum of (c) C1s, and (d) O1s of the R-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra at different pH levels, (b) PL emission spectra under different pH conditions at 310 nm excitation, (c) zeta potential at different pH levels, and (d) FT-IR spectra at pH 2.15 (red line) and pH 12.01 (black line) of R-CDs in DI water. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Schematic representation of possible prototropic equilibria at different certain pH range, and (b) corresponding bright-field images of as-prepared R-CDs under different pH conditions.

(a) PL emission response in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions from top to bottom (0–30 μM) (inset shows the linear relationship plot of (F0 − F)/F0 vs. Fe3+ ion concentration for R-CDs), (b) selectivity of R-CDs in the presence of various metal ions as potential interferences at pH 7 ± 0.1 in DI water, (c) PL emission spectra of R-CDs at different KCl

concentrations, and (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs (black line) and R-CDs-Fe3+ mixture (red line) at pH 7 ± 0.1 in DI water. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of plausible PL quenching mechanism in the presence of Fe3+ ions in DI water.
Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of R-CDs and the deprotonation-induced fluorescence switch in different certain pH range as well as the detection of Fe3+ ions at pH 7 ± 0.1.

head.ce:title: Mechanistic studies on the β-resorcylic acid mediated carbon dots for the pH-induced fluorescence switch and sensing application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, Seongnam 1342, Seongnam-daero, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do, 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) responsive to external stimuli that cause changes in their optical properties are in high demand. Herein, we present a microwave synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs derived for the first time from β-resorcylic acid (henceforth referred as R-CDs) in sulfuric acid (H2SO4) medium. Interestingly, the as-synthesized R-CDs showed tunable optical properties with three distinguishable colors (green/blue/blue-violet) depending on pH under UV irradiation. Spectral analyses and mechanistic studies revealed that the fluorescence switch is due to the deprotonation of the oxygenous surface functionalities in the R-CDs. The pH-dependent color switchability was tested in a number of cycles under UV light by adding 0.1 (M) HCl and NaOH. Although several mechanisms involving the complex photoluminescence of CDs have been reported, we propose here that the photoluminescence behavior of the CDs can be tuned by deprotonation of the oxygenous surface functionalities. Moreover, these R-CDs can be used as a potential visual indicator of three different pH ranges, and as highly selective and sensitive sensing medium for Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.43 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as new members of the carbon family, have emerged as excellent multifunctional materials that have been extensively studied for last few years because of their high photostability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and ease of synthesis from green natural resources or less toxic laboratory chemicals [1–5]. CDs have found widespread use in sensing [6], photocatalysis [7], bioimaging [8], photovoltaics [9], light-emitting diodes [10], and antibacterial agents [11] because of their superior properties as compared to those of semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes. Various methods for synthesizing CDs have been proposed, such as the hydrothermal method [12], chemical oxidation [13], electrochemical ablation [14], carbonization of organic compounds [15], and microwave irradiation [16]. Among these, microwave irradiation is the most popular because it requires a short time and low temperature. Sulfuric acid being a strong dehydrating oxidant, is often used to synthesize carbon dots because it helps in dehydration induced aggregation and carbonization. For example, V.M. Naik et al. reported low cost and quick method for the synthesis of carbon dots using sulfuric acid as dehydrating acid [17]. Jun Wang et al. reported carbon dots synthesis from benzenediol isomers by simple microwave treatment using sulfuric acid as dehydrating agent [18].

CDs responsive to external stimuli, which cause changes in their optical properties, are highly desired and have received much attention owing to their multifaceted applications, especially in sensing technology. Development of such fluorescent materials that are highly sensitive to pH is greatly encouraged. Yuan et al. reported multicolor fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which were colorimetrically and fluorometrically responsive to pH, thus showing great potential for application to pH detection in complex biological media [19]. Song et al. reported N, S co-doped CDs with pH-dependent fluorescence switchability and high selectivity for glutathione [20]. Fan et al. reported fluorescent graphene quantum dots that showed a distinct response at pH 6.8, with a sharp fluorescence switch from green to blue [21]. Although CDs show similar optical properties, various deliberations have been constantly dedicated to elucidate the underlying photoluminescence emission mechanisms, such as quantum confinement [22], aggregation-induced emission [23], presence of a multichromophoric group [24], presence of molecular fluorophores [25], surface trap emission [26], crystalline and amorphous carbon clusters [27], and variable oxidation states [28]. Nonetheless, an in-depth understanding of the photoluminescence behavior is lacking. An interesting feature of CDs is that their surface can be decorated with different types of functional groups (COOH, OH, C=O, and NH2) depending on the source materials and synthesis procedures; these functional groups can strongly influence the PL emission behavior by interaction with external stimuli. Shi et al. reported that with decrease in pH value below a critical acidic pH, the intensity of the emission peak decreases with a red shift due to deprotonation [29].

On the other hand, Fe3+ being an essential element, acts in several cellular functions such as oxygen and CO2 transport, electron transfer, hemoglobin formation, regulation of transcription, cellular metabolism, cellular respiration, DNA repair, enzymatic reactions, and bio-syntheses etc. Low intake of bioavailable iron causes most common nutritional deficiency syndrome, anemia. Nucleic acids and proteins are damaged due to excessive iron accumulation in living cell [30]. So it is very much important to sense iron quantitatively and qualitatively based on sophisticated fluorescence systems.

Herein, we successfully developed a CD-based novel dual functional sensor that can be utilized as a visual pH indicator as well as a highly selective and sensitive sensor for Fe3+ ions. The plausible mechanism of fluorescence with pH and Fe3+ detection is also discussed.

2,4-Dihydroxybenzoic acid or β-resorcylic acid (C7H6O4), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), 1-butanol (C4H9OH), and all metal chlorides/nitrates (AgNO3, BaCl2·2H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, CeCl2·7H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CsCl, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, In(NO3)3·6H2O, KCl, La(NO3)3·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NaCl, NH4Cl, NiCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2·2H2O, and FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Deionized (DI) water was used for all the experiments. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

A Varian Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to record ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a high-resolution Bruker Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) observations of the as-prepared R-CDs were carried out using a JEM 3010 microscope (JEOL Ltd., JAPAN) at 300 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a Sigma Probe spectrometer (Thermo VG), equipped with an Al-Kα source (15 kV, 100 W, 400 m). X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies were carried out in a Scintag-SDS 2000 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation operating at 40 kV and 30 mA. A Thermo Scientific pH meter was used to record the pH. All PL measurements were carried out at room temperature using a fluoroluminescence spectrometer (Quanta Master, Photon Technology International, NJ, USA) with a Xe lamp (Arc Lamp Housing, A-1010BTM), monochromator, and power supply (Brytexbox). The surface charge of the as-synthesized R-CDs was determined using a Photal Otsuka Electronics zeta potential analyzer. The quantum yield measurement was carried out with the Quantum Yield System (Fluoro-Q2100) at 350 nm excitation using the quinine sulfate as reference. Absorbance value of sample (carbon dots in water) and reference (quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4) was kept absorbance at 0.06. Finally, quantum yield data was taken in Fluoro-Q2100 instrument using the following equation:
(1)
Q u a n t u m
y i e l d =
E
c
(
L
a
−
L
c
)

Where Ec and La correspond to the emission due to direct excitation and the total amount of excitation light, respectively. Lc corresponds to the amount of light measured after direct excitation [31]. A 1% LUDOX® LS colloidal silica suspension in water was used for the instrument response function (IRF) of the background signal in lifetime decay measurements. All fluorescence lifetime decay curves were recorded using an EasyLife II fluorometer system (Photon Technology International), with auto-adjustable excitation wavelengths ranging from 260 to 650 nm. The curve fitting data were obtained using the EasyLife II program via a serial RS232 and USB connection.

The R-CDs were synthesized by a facile microwave irradiation method reported elsewhere [18], with slight modification using β-resorcylic acid in concentrated H2SO4 medium. In a typical procedure, β-resorcylic acid (1 g) was added to concentrated H2SO4 acid (10 mL) in a beaker, which was placed on a controlled heating platform and heated constantly at 80 °C for 45 min with stirring. As the reaction progressed, the colorless solution became faint yellow. The solution was transferred to a microwave glass bottle equipped with a reflux condenser and thermometer. Microwave irradiation at 500 W with constant stirring and at a constant temperature (130 °C) for 1 h led to dehydration-induced aggregation and gave the desired brownish yellow solution of R-CDs. The solution was cooled naturally to room temperature, and the R-CDs were extracted several times using a 1-butanol/water (1:1 by volume) mixture. The R-CDs extracted into the 1-butanol layer were dried at 70 °C and finally dispersed in water. The yield of the product was calculated to be 50–80 mg.

0.1 mg/mL suspensions of the R-CDs were adjusted to the desired pH level using roughly 0.1 (M) HCl and NaOH. The fluorescence spectra of these suspensions were recorded at excitation wavelengths of 310 and 350 nm.

The R-CDs were adjusted to a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL at pH 7 ± 0.1, and 1 mM of aqueous solutions containing various environmentally/biologically relevant metal ions (Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cs2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, In3+, K+, La3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, NH4 +, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+) were prepared. To evaluate the selectivity of the R-CDs, 200 μL of each metal ion solution was mixed with 2800 μL of the R-CD suspension, and the fluorescence spectra were measured. To evaluate the sensitivity to Fe3+, 100 μL of the R-CD suspension was added to 2900 μL of Fe3+ solution with various concentrations (0–30 μM). The control sample was prepared by mixing 100 μL of R-CD suspension with 2900 μL of DI water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism and morphological analysis of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The R-CDs were prepared using the microwave irradiation method using H2SO4 as both the dehydrating agent and the solvent [18,32]. A few recent reports revealed that the presence of hydroxyl groups at the meta position to each other are the key for the formation of CDs via dehydration-induced aggregation mediated by H2SO4 or an oxidizing agent [32,33]. The plausible formation mechanism of the R-CDs may be suggested according to Scheme 1 . In the H2SO4 environment, the hydroxyl groups can participate in the elimination of water to form an aggregated structure of R-CDs, as shown in Scheme 1. The dehydration-induced aggregation reaction finally leads to CDs with carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups.

The structure and morphology of the R-CDs were investigated by HR-TEM and XRD. According to the TEM images in Fig. 1 a and b, the R-CDs are spherical and well dispersed. Gaussian fitting curves were plotted from the TEM images of the R-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1c. Accordingly, the average size of the R-CDs was 8.5–9.5 nm, which is close to that of the previously reported CDs prepared by direct pyrolysis using citric acid and ammonia [34]. The HR-TEM images of R-CDs showed a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.32 nm (Fig. 1a), which was consistent with that for the (002) diffraction facets of graphite [35]. The analogous XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) displayed a broad (002) peak of graphite centered at 25.5°, which was in good agreement with HR-TEM analysis and similar to that of CDs fabricated by other methods [35,36]. These results clearly implied the formation of R-CDs by graphitization using the microwave reaction. The thermal stability and surface functional groups weight loss of R-CDs were determined by TGA analysis (Fig. S1). R-CDs show an initial weight loss till 154 °C (9.945%) which may be attributed to the loss of water molecules and surface O-H functional groups. The second weight loss was observed between 154 and 240 °C (9.066%), which may be corresponding to COOH acid group decomposition on the surface. Finally, the last step decomposition was occurred beyond >240 °C representing the start point of decomposition of aliphatic and aromatic domains and at temperature >300 °C R-CDs were left with simple amorphous carbon due to combustion [37,38].

The surface functional groups of R-CDs were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the peak at around 3175-3445 cm−1 in the spectrum of the R-CDs could be ascribed to O-H stretching vibration [35]. The peaks at 1712 cm−1 and 1602 cm−1 were attributed to C=O and C=C stretching, respectively [40,41]. The peaks at 1050–1200 cm−1 and 1280 cm−1 could be assigned to C-O-C/C-C and C-OH stretching, respectively [18,35,42]. The peaks at 2950 cm−1, 2865 cm−1, and 1370 cm−1 were related to the asymmetric stretching (νas), symmetric stretching (νs), and symmetric bending (δs) vibrations of the C-H bond [43]. The surface composition of the R-CDs was also investigated by XPS measurements (Fig. 3b–d). As shown in Fig. 3b, the spectrum contains carbon (285 eV) and oxygen 532 (eV) peaks [44]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) showed three main peaks at 283.6, 285.4, and 287.04 eV, which could be ascribed to (C=C, C-C), (C-O), and C=O, respectively [18]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) showed three peaks centered at 531.4, 532.6, and 536.9 eV, which could be attributed to C=O, C–O–C/C–OH, and O=C–OH [45], respectively. The results of FTIR and XPS analyses revealed that the R-CDs mainly contain hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent fluorescence switching and its probable mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To date, there have been a few studies on the pH-induced fluorescence switch in CDs [20,21,46–48]. The R-CDs showed pH-sensitive performance at different pH levels. The pH-induced changes in the spectral properties of these R-CDs provide deep insight into the chemistry of the surface functional groups responsible for PL emission shift and the underlying mechanism. As shown in Fig. 4 a, interesting features were observed in the absorption spectra of the aqueous dispersion of R-CDs under different pH conditions (1, 2.3, 3.4, 3.9, 4.8, 5.8, 6.8, 7.3, 8.3, 9.5, 10.5, 11.3, and 12). Notably, the absorption bands at 234 nm and 287 nm remained almost unchanged when the pH was increased up to 8.3, but at pH 9.5, both the bands started decreasing in intensity; then, two new bands gradually appeared at longer wavelengths of 256 nm and 298 nm. These pH-induced spectral changes were completely reversible, indicating no degradation of the R-CDs at high pH.

There are several reports regarding the effect of pH on the absorption spectra of CDs. Konkena et al. reported that the PL of CDs was pH-dependent, but there was no significant change in the absorption spectra [49]. Conversely, Pan et al. and Zheng et al. reported a blue shift in the absorption and emission spectra of CDs, along with a decrease in the PL intensity at low pH [50,51]. Recently, Choudhury et al. reported a gradual pH-dependent change in the absorption spectra due to the formation of chemically different CD species with different electronic energy levels, which in turn was attributed to the deprotonation of oxygenous surface functionalities [46]. The present work supports the fact that the pH conditions can notably modify the energy levels, and consequently, control the type of electronic transitions occurring in R-CDs. This effect is possibly due to deprotonation of the oxygen-containing surface functionalities (OH and COOH) of R-CDs, resulting in the formation of chemically different R-CD species in solutions with different pH levels.

The effects of pH were also reflected in the PL spectra of the R-CDs (Fig. 4b). The PL spectra at different pH levels (1, 2, 2.8, 3.6, 4.2, 4.7, 6, 7, 8.15, 9.2, 10.7, 11.5, 12, and 12.4) were recorded with excitation at 310 nm. The PL maximum was observed at 494 and 436 nm for pH 1 and pH 12.4, respectively. The interesting feature of the R-CDs is that the emission intensities gradually increase with the increase of pH up to pH 7 and then decrease at higher pH; this is accompanied by a significant but gradual blue shift of the emission spectra with a sudden change in two regions, pH 2–4 and pH 8–10. This sudden blue shift has been observed for the first time in β-resorcylic acid-derived R-CDs. The inset of Fig. 4b shows the emission wavelength maximum vs. pH curve, which is sigmoidal, with two inflection points at 3.04 and 8.9. The figures clearly indicate that at different pH levels, chemically different species are formed, probably due to deprotonation of the oxygen-containing surface functionalities. Similar results were obtained from UV–vis absorption spectra, which revealed a dramatic change at pH 8.3–9.5. The combined results deduced from the PL emission spectra and UV absorption spectra under different pH conditions confirmed that both prototropic equilibria (carboxylic ↔ carboxylate and phenol ↔ phenolate) strongly affect the emission and absorption of CDs, and thus influence the electronic energy levels associated with the electronic transitions [46,52].

For further interpretation of the pH-dependent properties of R-CDs, zeta potential measurements were performed under different pH (2, 7, and 12) conditions; the corresponding values were 0.49 mV, −8.11 mV, and −19.04 mV (Fig. 4c), which were in accordance with the pH-induced deprotonation of the surface functionalities. The positive potential close to zero under acidic condition (ξ = 0.49 mV at pH 2) suggested that all oxygenous surface functional groups are protonated, while negative potentials at pH 7 (ξ = −8.11) and pH 12 (ξ = −19.04 mV) indicated the deprotonated form of the COOH and OH groups, respectively. CHN analysis was performed in different pH medium (pH-2, pH-7, pH-12) to confirm deprotonation as shown in Table. S1. The deprotonation can be realized from the C:H ratio at pH −2, pH −7, pH −12 which were 18:1.01, 18:0.92, 18:0.85 respectively. The present observations clarified that at a fixed excitation wavelength, the shift in the PL emission spectra of the R-CDs at varying pH originated from the existence of various charged R-CDs species in the medium depending on protonation and deprotonation.

FTIR spectra were also recorded under different pH conditions to study the pH-induced changes in the R-CDs (Fig. 4d). Upon increasing the pH of the aqueous dispersion from 2.15 to 12.01, the peak at 1712 cm−1 due to the C=O stretching of the undissociated carboxylic acid nearly disappeared, and two new peaks appeared at 1544 cm−1 and 1474 cm−1, which were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of the carboxylate group, respectively [53,54]. The peak intensity for O-H stretching around 3178–3445 cm−1 reduced dramatically, and a new peak was observed at 1292 cm−1, probably due to the deprotonated C-OH [46]. To study the structural stability with respect to pH, the XRD patterns were recorded at different pH medium as shown in Fig. S3 [55].

It is known that the carboxylic group is more acidic (pKa 3–5) than the phenolic OH group (pKa 8–10). Thus, it is reasonable to state that the pKa values of 3.04 and 8.9 observed from the pH-induced changes in the emission spectra of R-CDs (Fig. 4b) correspond to the carboxylic acid ↔ carboxylate and phenol ↔ phenolate deprotonation equilibria, respectively [46,52].

The above mentioned investigations clarify that deprotonation of both the oxygenous surface functional groups occurs under high-pH conditions. The sigmoidal curve reveals the existence of two prototropic equilibria in the system, with two inflection points corresponding to the two different pKa values observed in the pH-dependent emission studies (Fig. 4b). Therefore, the photophysical properties of the R-CDs are actively determined by both oxygenous surface functional groups (COOH, OH). A schematic of the different prototropic equilibria between the R-CDs under different pH conditions is depicted in Fig. 5 a. In addition, Fig. 5b shows the bright-field image of the R-CDs, which exhibit a significant color change from green to blue-violet over the pH range 1–12 under UV illumination in DI water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Among heavy metals, iron plays an important role in several organic and biological processes in vivo, such as hemoglobin formation, oxygen transport, electron transfer, cellular metabolism, enzymatic reactions, and bio-syntheses [56]. As a fluorescent material, CDs have recently found extensive use for the selective and sensitive detection of various heavy and toxic metal ions.

To examine the PL sensitivity of the R-CDs toward Fe3+, fluorescence quenching measurements were performed at different Fe3+ concentrations in the range 0–30 μM. The R-CDs were adjusted to pH 7 ± 0.1, and sensing experiments were carried out. Upon the addition of 30 μM of Fe3+ ions to the dispersion of R-CDs, 86% quenching of the fluorescence emission was observed (Fig. 6 a). The fluorescence spectra of the aqueous dispersions of R-CDs with different Fe3+ concentrations (0, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 20, 25, and 30 μM) are shown in Fig. 6a. The addition of increasing concentrations of Fe3+ ions led to a gradual decrease in the PL (excitation wavelength: 350 nm). Although the plot of (F0-F/F0) vs. Fe3+ concentration was not perfectly linear over the range 0–30 μM, excellent linearity was observed at 0–12.5 μM, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9992 as shown in the inset of Fig. 6a. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.43 μM (3 times σ/K). The LOD provided by the R-CDs is much smaller than the limit of Fe3+ concentration in drinking water (∼5.357 μM) set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [57].

Further selectivity studies were carried out between Fe3+ and other environmentally/biologically relevant metal ions. For this purpose, several interfering metal ions such as Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cs2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, In3+, K+, La3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, NH4 +, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+ were chosen. Fig. 6b (inset) shows the photograph of the R-CDs before and after the addition of various metal ions under UV illumination. The selectivity of the proposed Fe3+ PL sensor was evaluated, as described in section 2.5. First, 200 μL of each metal ion solution was mixed with 2800 μL of the R-CD suspension, and the fluorescence spectra were measured to record the fluorescence intensity change. The fluorescence quenching ability of different metal ions is shown in Fig. 6b. The R-CD sensor shows higher selectivity for Fe3+ than for the other metal ions. Although the mechanism of selectivity toward Fe3+ in the presence of other metal ions is presently unknown, it is presumed that Fe3+ being a hard acid metal ion always prefers hard bases such as oxygen-containing surface moieties. This hard acid-hard base coordination interaction facilitates easier adsorption of Fe3+ on the surface of the R-CDs as compared to that of the other metal ions. Moreover, the coordination interaction assists non-radiative electron transfer from the conduction band of the R-CDs to the vacant d-orbital of Fe3+ in the excited state, owing to electron-hole recombination, thereby leading to fluorescence quenching. These results demonstrate that the R-CDs have very good sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ ions.

The effect of salt on the fluorescence properties of the R-CDs was also investigated (Fig. 6c). Various concentrations of KCl (0–2 mol/L) were mixed with the R-CDs, and the corresponding PL spectra were recorded. The PL intensity of the RCDs was stable even at high KCl concentration (2 mol/L), implying that the R-CDs can be used in the presence of high salt concentrations.

Photoluminescence quenching by Fe3+ is believed to be due to the fast electron transfer from the CDs to the Fe3+ ions. Fe3+ can be easily absorbed on the surface of CDs containing phenolic hydroxyl groups at their edges, possibly because of the hard acid-hard base interaction [58].

The coordination interaction of Fe3+ with the oxygenous surface functional groups was confirmed by UV–Vis absorption experiments before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions to the R-CDs (Fig. 6d). Upon the addition of Fe3+ to the R-CDs, a new peak appeared at around 483 nm and the peak at 234 nm decreased simultaneously. To further confirm the coordination interaction of Fe3+ on the surface, zeta potential measurements were carried out at pH 7 (Fig. S5). The zeta potential of the R-CDs at pH 7 was −8.11 mV, which dramatically changed to 5.1 mV after the addition of Fe3+ ions. The change in the zeta potential from negative to positive indicated that the Fe3+ ions coordinate with the oxygenous surface functional groups. As a result, electrons were transferred from the conduction band of the R-CDs to the unfilled d-orbitals of Fe3+ in a non-radiative manner, leading to fluorescence quenching. In addition, we investigate the structural stability of R-CDs before and after Fe3+ ions treatment as represented in Fig. S4, and significant changes were not observed from this study. To further support the sensing mechanism, we performed a fluorescence quenching spectra with various concentration of Fe3+ at different temperatures and we analyzed the PL intensity data by Stern-Volmer (S-V) equation.
(2)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[
Q
]
where F0 and F represents PL intensities in absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. KSV and [Q] represents Stern-Volmer constant and quencher concentration respectively. Fig. S6 shows the plot of F0/F versus analyte concentrations [Q]. Using slope, the Ksv values were calculated. Decrease in Ksv values were observed with increase in temperature (T) which suggests that at higher temperature complex formation were less efficient at the surface. Dissociation of loosely bound complexes at higher temperature reveals that the quenching behavior were probably static in nature

For a deeper insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, time-resolved lifetime decay analysis was performed in the presence and absence of Fe3+ in the R-CDs. Fig. S7 shows the fluorescence decay profile, which is well fitted by the double exponential function according to the following equation:

(
− t
τ
2
. )
where R (t) is the sum of individual single exponential decays, B1 and B2 are the pre-exponential factors, τ1 and τ2 are the decay times. The average lifetime τave of the R-CDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ was calculated according to the following equation:
(4)
τ
a v e
=
B
1
τ
1
2
+
B
2
τ
2
2
B
1
τ
1
+
B
2
τ
2

The best fitted lifetimes are presented in Table S2. τave of the R-CDs at pH 7 ± 0.1was calculated to be 5.62 ns, but upon the addition of Fe3+, this value decayed to 5.55 ns. The slight decrease in fluorescence life time indicated that the PL quenching might have occurred due to static quenching owing to the formation of nonfluorescent stable ground state complex between R-CDs and Fe3+. According to the above mentioned analysis, a plausible working mechanism for the coordination between the hydroxyl and carboxylic groups of the R-CDs and Fe3+ ions is proposed (Fig. 7 ).

In summary, a simple, rapid, and low-cost microwave irradiation method was successfully adopted to synthesize R-CDs with a quantum yield of ∼21%, for the first time from commonly available β-resorcylic acid using H2SO4 as the dehydrating catalyst. Interestingly, the as-synthesized R-CDs showed pH-induced tunable optical properties with three distinguishable colors (green, blue, and blue-violet) depending on pH range. The reason for the fluorescence switch was investigated using several characterization tools, which revealed that two prototropic equilibria (carboxylic acid ↔ carboxylate and phenol ↔ phenolate) are the major contributors for the photophysical properties associated with electronic energy transitions. We believe that the as-obtained R-CDs can be practically utilized as a visual pH indicator. Moreover, the R-CDs can be used as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions at pH 7 ± 0.1, with an LOD of 0.43 μM.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the GRRC program of Gyeonggi Province (GRRC Gachon 2017-B01), and by the Human Resource Development of the Korea Insitute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (20174030201530).
